narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 69% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, leading to the rejection. This prediction is based on patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on factors like academic performance, test scores, and background that best indicate bar exam success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is in the bottom tier compared to the average of around the 5th or 6th decile; this suggests your later high school or early college grades placed you lower in your peer group than most who pass the bar. Next in importance was your undergraduate GPA, which actually worked in your favor—its strength above the norm pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass, counteracting some risks. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 1 decile of 1 also nudged the outlook toward failure, again reflecting a lower starting rank relative to peers who typically succeed. Other elements, like your LSAT score of 33 (below the 36.7 average), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—which is common for 93% of applicants—played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers. Compared to typical admitted students, your early academic deciles are a bigger red flag, even with your strong college record, as the model weighs consistent performance across stages heavily for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background; it's the model spotting patterns where low early deciles often correlate with bar struggles regardless of later improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, gathering stronger references that highlight your GPA resilience, or exploring programs with holistic reviews that might value your full-time commitment more. You could also audit courses or prep programs to demonstrate upward trends in performance, addressing those early decile concerns head-on. Many students in similar spots reapply successfully after targeted improvements—your solid UGPA gives you a great foundation to build on. Keep pushing; this is just one model's snapshot of your path.
